parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Lord Ashton of Hyde (Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State, DCMS)
commonsSponsors: Matt Hancock (Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Most provisions commenced on 25 May 2018 to coincide with the GDPR coming into force. Certain provisions on age-appropriate design codes commenced later.

secondReadingLords.summary: Lord Ashton of Hyde introduced the Bill as making UK data protection laws fit for the digital age. Twenty years since the DPA 1998, the explosive growth of the world wide web, social media, and smartphones required a comprehensive update. The Bill supplemented the directly applicable GDPR with UK-specific provisions.

secondReadingCommons.summary: Matt Hancock moved the Second Reading, framing the Bill within Parliament's tradition of working with rather than against technology — from the Electric Lighting Act 1882 to the Television Act 1954. The Bill was debated during the Cambridge Analytica scandal, which significantly intensified parliamentary scrutiny of data protection enforcement powers.

significantCommitteeAmendments.summary: Controversial exemption allowing data subject rights to be restricted where exercise would prejudice effective immigration control. Extensively debated in both Houses. Later found incompatible with UK GDPR by Court of Appeal in Open Rights Group & others v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2021] EWCA Civ 1573 and subsequently amended by SI 2024/267.
significantCommitteeAmendments.outcome: Enacted but subsequently amended following judicial challenge

significantCommitteeAmendments.summary: Requirement for the ICO to produce a code of practice on age-appropriate design of information society services likely to be accessed by children. Significant cross-party support. Led to the influential Age Appropriate Design Code published September 2020.

significantCommitteeAmendments.amendment: Enhanced enforcement powers following Cambridge Analytica
significantCommitteeAmendments.summary: Government amendments at Commons Report stage significantly strengthened the Information Commissioner's enforcement powers in response to the Cambridge Analytica investigation. The Commissioner's office had described the existing enforcement powers under the DPA 1998 as insufficient for the scale of investigation required.
significantCommitteeAmendments.outcome: Enacted — enforcement notice and penalty provisions strengthened

precedingDocuments.title: Data Protection Act 1998
precedingDocuments.significance: The DPA 2018 repealed and replaced the DPA 1998 in its entirety. The 1998 Act had implemented EU Directive 95/46/EC.

precedingDocuments.title: EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) 2016/679
precedingDocuments.significance: The DPA 2018 supplements the directly applicable GDPR (now retained as the UK GDPR post-Brexit). The Act cannot be understood without reading alongside the UK GDPR.

precedingDocuments.title: EU Law Enforcement Directive 2016/680
precedingDocuments.significance: Part 3 of the DPA 2018 transposes the LED into UK law for law enforcement processing.

precedingDocuments.title: European Convention on Human Rights Article 8
precedingDocuments.significance: The right to respect for private and family life underpins the entire data protection framework. DPA 2018 s.2(2) expressly references the Convention right.

parliamentaryOrigin.cambridgeAnalyticaContext: The Bill's passage through Parliament coincided with the Cambridge Analytica scandal (March 2018). The ICO's investigation into Facebook and Cambridge Analytica was unprecedented in scale and directly influenced Government amendments to strengthen enforcement powers at Commons Report stage. Matt Hancock stated the Government would ensure the Commissioner had 'all the powers she needs.'

leadingCases.name: WM Morrison Supermarkets plc v Various Claimants
leadingCases.principle: Employer not vicariously liable for rogue employee's deliberate data breach where employee was pursuing a personal vendetta, not furthering employer's business. However, the principle of vicarious liability CAN in principle apply to breaches of data protection law by employees.
leadingCases.heldText: The wrongful conduct of Mr Skelton was not so closely connected with acts which he was authorised to do that, for the purposes of Morrisons' liability to third parties, it can fairly and properly be regarded as done by him while acting in the ordinary course of his employment. He was pursuing a personal vendetta, seeking vengeance for disciplinary proceedings some months earlier.
leadingCases.significance: CRITICAL — the first UK class-action-type data breach claim. Established that vicarious liability for data protection breaches by employees is possible in principle, but not where the employee is acting against the employer's interests. Decided under DPA 1998 but principle applies equally under DPA 2018.

leadingCases.name: Lloyd v Google LLC
leadingCases.principle: Representative action under CPR 19.6 for data protection breaches requires proof of individual damage — 'loss of control' of personal data alone, without proof of financial loss or distress, is insufficient for a uniform per capita damages claim.
leadingCases.heldText: The claim could not be pursued as a representative action because it required individual assessment of whether each class member had suffered damage, and a claim for 'loss of control' damages was not sustainable under the DPA 1998.
leadingCases.significance: Major — significantly limited the scope for mass data breach class actions in the UK by requiring individual proof of damage rather than a uniform per capita award.

leadingCases.name: Vidal-Hall v Google Inc
leadingCases.principle: Compensation for breach of data protection law can be awarded for distress alone, without the need to prove financial loss. Section 13 of the DPA 1998 must be read as permitting this to comply with EU law.
leadingCases.heldText: The restriction in s.13(2) DPA 1998 to non-pecuniary damage only where pecuniary damage had also been suffered was incompatible with the rights conferred by Directive 95/46/EC and must be disapplied.
leadingCases.significance: Established that distress-only compensation is available for data protection breaches. DPA 2018 s.168 expressly provides for compensation including for distress, reflecting this ruling.

leadingCases.name: Ittihadieh v 5-11 Cheyne Gardens RTM Company Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Subject access requests (SARs) are a right of the data subject and cannot be refused merely because the requester has an ulterior motive or the request is motivated by litigation.
leadingCases.heldText: The motive of the requester is irrelevant to the obligation to comply with a SAR. The right of access is absolute — subject only to exemptions within the Act itself, not to a judicial assessment of the requester's purpose.
leadingCases.significance: Critical for employers — employees frequently make SARs in the context of disputes or anticipated tribunal claims. The employer cannot refuse on grounds that the request is litigation-driven.

leadingCases.name: Dawson-Damer v Taylor Wessing LLP
leadingCases.principle: Legal professional privilege provides a lawful basis for withholding personal data from a SAR response. Schedule 2 Part 4 para 19 of the DPA 2018 codifies this exemption.
leadingCases.heldText: The exemption for legal professional privilege applies to SARs, and the holder of the privilege (the client, not the law firm) must consent to waiver before privileged material can be disclosed.
leadingCases.significance: Directly relevant to employers responding to employee SARs where legal advice has been taken about the employee — privileged communications can be withheld.

leadingCases.name: Halford v United Kingdom
leadingCases.principle: Intercepting an employee's workplace telephone calls without warning violates Article 8 ECHR. Employees have a reasonable expectation of privacy in workplace communications absent clear warning.
leadingCases.heldText: The applicant could have had a reasonable expectation that calls made from her office telephone were not intercepted. The absence of any warning that calls would be monitored created a reasonable expectation of privacy.
leadingCases.significance: Foundational for workplace monitoring law. Establishes that Article 8 applies to workplace communications and that employers must provide clear notice of monitoring.

leadingCases.name: Barbulescu v Romania
leadingCases.court: European Court of Human Rights (Grand Chamber)
leadingCases.principle: Employer monitoring of employee's personal communications at work, even in breach of a clear workplace policy, may violate Article 8 ECHR if the monitoring is disproportionate.
leadingCases.heldText: An employer's instructions cannot reduce private social life in the workplace to zero. National courts must assess whether the employee received prior notification, the extent of monitoring, whether the employer had legitimate reasons, whether less intrusive methods existed, the consequences for the employee, and whether adequate safeguards were in place.
leadingCases.significance: CRITICAL — established six-factor proportionality test for workplace monitoring that directly informs UK employer obligations under DPA 2018 and the ICO's Employment Practices Code.

leadingCases.name: Copland v United Kingdom
leadingCases.principle: Monitoring of an employee's email, internet usage, and telephone calls without a legal basis or adequate notice constitutes interference with Article 8 rights.
leadingCases.heldText: The applicant's email, internet usage, and telephone communications at work were covered by the notions of 'private life' and 'correspondence' within the meaning of Article 8. The monitoring was not 'in accordance with the law' as required by Article 8(2).
leadingCases.significance: Extended Halford to email and internet usage monitoring. Established that all electronic communications at work attract Article 8 protection.

parts.title: Part 1 — UK GDPR Integration and General Processing Framework

sections.title: Protection of personal data
sections.summary: Declaratory provision establishing that the Act protects individuals with regard to the processing of personal data, including by requiring processing to be lawful, fair, and transparent. Expressly references the Convention right to respect for private and family life under Article 8 ECHR.
sections.text:
(1) The GDPR, the applied GDPR and this Act protect individuals with regard to the processing of personal data, in particular by—

(a) requiring personal data to be processed lawfully and fairly, on the basis of the data subject's consent or another specified basis,

(b) conferring rights on the data subject to obtain information about the processing of personal data and to require inaccurate personal data to be rectified, and

(c) conferring functions on the Commissioner, giving the holder of that office responsibility for monitoring and enforcing their provisions.

(2) When carrying out functions under the GDPR, the applied GDPR and this Act, the Commissioner must have regard to the importance of securing an appropriate level of protection for personal data, taking account of the interests of data subjects, controllers and others and matters of general public interest.
sections.keyPrinciple: The DPA 2018 supplements and works alongside the UK GDPR — it cannot be read in isolation from the directly applicable regulation.
refs: UK GDPR Article 5 (processing principles)
ECHR Article 8 (right to private life)
s.3 DPA 2018 (definitions)
Vidal-Hall v Google Inc [2015] EWCA Civ 311 (compensation for distress)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Guide to the UK GDPR
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive ICO guidance on interpreting the UK GDPR principles that DPA 2018 supplements

sections.title: Terms relating to the processing of personal data
sections.summary: Master definitions section establishing the meaning of 'personal data', 'processing', 'controller', 'processor', 'data subject', and 'special categories of personal data' as they apply throughout the Act. Critical for employers because the definition of 'controller' determines primary data protection responsibility.
sections.text:
(1) This section defines terms used in this Act.

(2) 'Personal data' means any information relating to an identified or identifiable living individual (subject to subsection (14)(c)).

(3) 'Identifiable living individual' means a living individual who can be identified, directly or indirectly, in particular by reference to—

(a) an identifier such as a name, an identification number, location data or an online identifier, or

(b) one or more factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural or social identity of the individual.

(4) 'Processing', in relation to information, means an operation or set of operations which is performed on information, or on sets of information, such as—

(a) collection, recording, organisation, structuring or storage,

(b) adaptation or alteration,

(c) retrieval, consultation or use,

(d) disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available,

(e) alignment or combination, or

(f) restriction, erasure or destruction.

(5) 'Controller', in relation to the processing of personal data to which Chapter 2 or 3 of Part 2, Part 3 or Part 4 applies, means the person who determines the purposes and means of the processing of personal data.

(6) 'Processor', in relation to the processing of personal data to which Chapter 2 or 3 of Part 2, Part 3 or Part 4 applies, means any person who processes personal data on behalf of the controller (other than a person who is an employee of the controller).
sections.keyPrinciple: An employer who determines why and how employee personal data is processed is a 'controller' with primary data protection obligations.
refs: UK GDPR Article 4 (definitions)
s.2 DPA 2018 (protection of personal data)
Durant v FSA [2003] EWCA Civ 1746 (meaning of personal data)
Nowak v Data Protection Commissioner [2017] CJEU C-434/16 (exam scripts as personal data)

leadingCases.name: Durant v Financial Services Authority
leadingCases.principle: Personal data must be information that relates to the individual in a meaningful way — biographical significance test. Not every document mentioning a person's name constitutes personal data about that person.
leadingCases.heldText: Personal data is information that affects a person's privacy, whether in his personal or family life, business or professional capacity — the information must be 'biographical in a significant sense'.
leadingCases.significance: Critical for employer SAR responses — helps determine which documents mentioning an employee constitute their 'personal data'. The biographical significance test has been narrowed by later CJEU authority (Nowak) but remains relevant.

sections.title: Special categories of personal data and criminal convictions etc data
sections.summary: Introduces Schedule 1 conditions for processing special category data (health, race, religion, trade union membership, biometric data, sexual orientation) and criminal convictions data under the UK GDPR. Employers routinely process all of these categories in the employment context — occupational health, diversity monitoring, DBS checks, trade union deductions.
sections.text:
(1) Subsection (2) makes provision about the processing of personal data described in Article 9(1) of the GDPR (special categories of personal data) in reliance on Article 9(2)(b) (employment, social security and social protection), (g) (substantial public interest), (h) (health or social care), (i) (public health) or (j) (archiving, research and statistics).

(2) The processing meets the requirement in Article 9(2)(b), (h), (i) or (j) for authorisation by, or a basis in, the law of the United Kingdom or a part of the United Kingdom only if it meets a condition in Part 1 of Schedule 1.

(3) The processing meets the requirement in Article 9(2)(g) for a basis in the law of the United Kingdom or a part of the United Kingdom only if it meets a condition in Part 2 of Schedule 1.

(4) Subsection (5) makes provision about the processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences or related security measures that is not carried out under the control of official authority.

(5) The processing meets the requirement in Article 10 of the GDPR for authorisation by the law of the United Kingdom or a part of the United Kingdom only if it meets a condition in Part 1, 2 or 3 of Schedule 1.
sections.keyPrinciple: Employers processing employee health data, trade union membership, criminal records, or diversity monitoring data must satisfy BOTH an Article 6 lawful basis AND a Schedule 1 condition.
refs: UK GDPR Article 9 (special categories)
UK GDPR Article 10 (criminal conviction data)
Schedule 1 DPA 2018 (conditions for sensitive processing)
Schedule 1 Part 1 para 1 (employment condition)
ICO Employment Practices Code

leadingCases.name: ICO Enforcement Notice — Serco Leisure Operating Limited
leadingCases.principle: Using biometric data (facial recognition and fingerprint scanning) for employee attendance monitoring is disproportionate where less intrusive alternatives exist. Biometric data is special category data under Article 9.
leadingCases.heldText: Serco Leisure and associated trusts were ordered to stop processing biometric data for attendance monitoring. The ICO found the processing was neither necessary nor proportionate given alternative methods were available.
leadingCases.significance: First major ICO enforcement on employer biometric monitoring — established that convenience does not justify processing biometric special category data for attendance.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Special category data guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ICO guidance on the conditions and appropriate policy documents required for processing special category data in employment

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employment practices and data protection — monitoring workers
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Updated October 2023 guidance specifically addressing proportionality of workplace monitoring and biometric data collection

commonErrors: Processing employee health data without identifying both an Article 6 lawful basis and a Schedule 1 condition
Failing to have an Appropriate Policy Document in place when required by Schedule 1
Using consent as the lawful basis for processing employee special category data when the power imbalance in the employment relationship means consent is unlikely to be freely given
Collecting trade union membership data without a clear Schedule 1 condition
Using biometric data (fingerprints, facial recognition) for attendance monitoring without a DPIA and legitimate Schedule 1 condition — ICO enforcement action against Serco Leisure (2024)

versions.text: As originally enacted and currently in force.
versions.policyRationale: Implements the UK's derogations under GDPR Articles 9 and 10, providing domestic conditions for processing sensitive data categories.

sections.title: Lawful bases for processing — employer obligations
sections.summary: The UK GDPR Article 6 requires every instance of personal data processing to have a lawful basis. For employers, this means every collection, storage, use, and sharing of employee data must be justified under one of six lawful bases. The most commonly relied-upon bases in employment are: legitimate interests (Art.6(1)(f)), performance of a contract (Art.6(1)(b)), legal obligation (Art.6(1)(c)), and — rarely appropriate due to the power imbalance — consent (Art.6(1)(a)).
sections.keyPrinciple: Consent is generally NOT the appropriate lawful basis for processing employee data because of the inherent power imbalance in the employment relationship — employees cannot freely refuse consent to their employer. Legitimate interests or legal obligation are usually more appropriate.
refs: UK GDPR Recital 47 (legitimate interests balancing test)
UK GDPR Article 7 (conditions for consent)
Article 29 Working Party Opinion 2/2017 on data processing at work
ICO Lawful basis guidance for employment
s.10 DPA 2018 (special categories require ADDITIONAL Article 9 condition)

leadingCases.name: Article 29 Working Party Opinion 2/2017 on data processing at work
leadingCases.principle: Consent should not be used as the lawful basis for processing employee data because the power imbalance inherent in the employment relationship means employees cannot freely refuse. The Opinion identified legitimate interests and legal obligation as the primary appropriate bases for employer processing.
leadingCases.heldText: Given the dependency that results from the employer/employee relationship, it is unlikely that the data subject is able to deny his/her employer consent to data processing without experiencing the fear or real risk of detrimental effects as a result of a refusal.
leadingCases.significance: CRITICAL — the definitive regulatory guidance on why consent fails as a lawful basis in employment. Directly informs ICO approach. Binding on UK interpretation via retained EU law principles.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Lawful basis for processing — legitimate interests
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ICO three-part test for legitimate interests: purpose test (is there a legitimate interest?), necessity test (is the processing necessary?), balancing test (do the individual's interests override?)

commonErrors: Using consent as the lawful basis for processing employee data — the power imbalance means consent is unlikely to be freely given and can be withdrawn at any time, creating operational chaos
Failing to document the lawful basis before processing begins — Article 5(2) accountability principle requires this
Relying on legitimate interests without conducting a documented Legitimate Interest Assessment (LIA)
Applying a single lawful basis to all processing — different categories of employee data may require different bases
Failing to inform employees of the lawful basis relied upon — UK GDPR Articles 13/14 require this in the privacy notice

sections.title: Right of access — subject access requests (SARs) in employment
sections.summary: UK GDPR Article 15 gives every data subject the right to obtain confirmation as to whether personal data concerning them is being processed and, where so, access to the personal data and supplementary information. In the employment context, this means employees can request copies of all personal data their employer holds about them — including emails, performance reviews, investigation notes, CCTV footage, and HR records. The employer must respond within one calendar month, free of charge.
sections.keyPrinciple: The right of subject access is absolute — subject only to the specific exemptions in DPA 2018 Schedule 2. The motive of the requester is irrelevant. An employee making a SAR in anticipation of a tribunal claim is fully entitled to do so.
refs: DPA 2018 Schedule 2 Part 4 para 22 (negotiations exemption)
DPA 2018 Schedule 2 Part 4 para 23 (confidential references exemption)
DPA 2018 Schedule 2 Part 4 para 24 (management forecasting exemption)
Ittihadieh v 5-11 Cheyne Gardens [2017] EWCA Civ 121 (motive irrelevant)
Dawson-Damer v Taylor Wessing [2017] EWCA Civ 74 (legal privilege exemption)
UK GDPR Article 12 (one calendar month response deadline)

leadingCases.name: Ittihadieh v 5-11 Cheyne Gardens RTM Company Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The motive of the requester is irrelevant to the obligation to comply with a SAR. The right of access cannot be refused merely because the request is made in connection with litigation or a dispute.
leadingCases.heldText: The motive of the data subject in making a SAR is irrelevant. The right of access is conferred by statute and cannot be defeated by the controller's view of the requester's purpose.
leadingCases.significance: CRITICAL for employers — establishes that SARs from disgruntled employees or those contemplating tribunal claims cannot be refused on the basis of ulterior motive.

leadingCases.name: Hoponangan v Commissioner of Police of the Metropolis
leadingCases.principle: A controller must conduct reasonable and proportionate searches to locate personal data. The obligation is not to search every possible location but to make reasonable enquiries.
leadingCases.heldText: The controller must take reasonable steps to find the requested information. This does not require searching every possible location, but it does require a diligent and systematic approach.
leadingCases.significance: Relevant to employers who hold employee data across multiple systems (HR, email, Slack, shared drives) — reasonable searches of all relevant systems are required.

commonErrors: Failing to respond within the one calendar month deadline — extensions of up to two further months are available only for complex or numerous requests, and the individual must be informed within one month
Charging a fee for a SAR — the right is exercisable free of charge unless the request is manifestly unfounded or excessive
Requiring the employee to use a specific form — a SAR can be made verbally, by email, in a meeting, or by any other means
Failing to search all relevant systems — including personal email accounts, messaging apps, and physical files where these contain personal data about the requester
Redacting third-party data excessively — the obligation is to provide the requester's personal data, redacting only information that identifies third parties where disclosure would be unfair to them
Treating a SAR as a complaint or disciplinary trigger rather than a data protection right

sections.title: Obligation to appoint a data protection officer
sections.summary: Transposes the UK GDPR Article 37 requirement for certain controllers to designate a data protection officer. Employers must appoint a DPO where their core activities consist of processing operations which require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects on a large scale, or large-scale processing of special category data.
sections.text:
(1) Section 69 of the GDPR (designation of a data protection officer) applies to a competent authority as it applies to a controller or processor, and for that purpose a reference to the tasks of a controller or processor is a reference to the tasks of a competent authority.

[Note: The DPO requirement for general processing is contained in UK GDPR Articles 37-39, which are directly applicable. DPA 2018 s.45 extends the obligation to law enforcement processing under Part 3.]
sections.keyPrinciple: The DPO requirement for most employers flows from UK GDPR Article 37 (directly applicable), not from the DPA 2018 itself. The Act extends the requirement to law enforcement processing.
refs: UK GDPR Article 37 (designation of DPO)
UK GDPR Article 38 (position of DPO)
UK GDPR Article 39 (tasks of DPO)
ICO guidance on DPO designation
commonErrors: Assuming all employers must appoint a DPO — the obligation applies only where processing activities meet the Article 37 thresholds
Appointing a DPO who also holds a role that determines purposes and means of processing, creating a conflict of interest

sections.title: Schedule 1 Part 1 — Employment, social security and social protection condition
sections.summary: The primary condition for processing special category data in the employment context. Permits processing where it is necessary for the purposes of performing or exercising obligations or rights which are imposed or conferred by law on the controller or the data subject in connection with employment. Requires an appropriate policy document to be in place.
sections.text:
(1) This condition is met if the processing—

(a) is necessary for the purposes of performing or exercising obligations or rights which are imposed or conferred by law on the controller or the data subject in connection with employment, social security or social protection, and

(b) when the processing is carried out, an appropriate policy document is in place (see paragraph 39 of this Schedule).

(2) See also the additional safeguards in Part 4 of this Schedule.
sections.keyPrinciple: This is the most commonly relied-upon condition for processing employee special category data. An Appropriate Policy Document MUST be in place before processing begins — failure to have one is a standalone compliance failure.
refs: UK GDPR Article 9(2)(b) (employment processing derogation)
Schedule 1 Part 4 para 39 (appropriate policy document requirements)
s.10 DPA 2018 (special categories)
ICO template appropriate policy document

regulatoryGuidance.title: Appropriate policy document template
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ICO template and guidance for the mandatory policy document required under Schedule 1 Part 4 paragraph 39

commonErrors: Processing employee health data, diversity monitoring data, or trade union membership without an Appropriate Policy Document in place
Treating the Appropriate Policy Document as optional — it is a legal requirement under Schedule 1
Failing to review and update the Appropriate Policy Document when processing activities change

sections.title: Schedule 2 Part 4 — Exemption: management forecasts and management planning
sections.summary: Exempts personal data processed for the purposes of management forecasting or management planning from the right of access (SAR) to the extent that compliance would prejudice the conduct of the business. Directly relevant to employers — protects redundancy planning, restructuring proposals, and succession planning documents from early disclosure via employee SARs.
sections.text: (1) The listed GDPR provisions do not apply to personal data processed for the purposes of management forecasting or management planning to assist the controller in the conduct of any business or other activity to the extent that the application of those provisions would be likely to prejudice the conduct of that business or other activity.
sections.keyPrinciple: Employers can withhold from employee SAR responses personal data contained in redundancy planning, restructuring proposals, or succession planning documents where disclosure would prejudice the business.
refs: UK GDPR Article 15 (right of access / SAR)
Schedule 2 Part 4 para 22 (negotiations exemption)
Schedule 2 Part 4 para 23 (confidential references exemption)
Ittihadieh v 5-11 Cheyne Gardens [2017] EWCA Civ 121 (SAR obligations)
commonErrors: Applying the management forecasting exemption too broadly — it requires genuine prejudice to the business, not merely inconvenience
Failing to consider whether redaction rather than wholesale exemption would be proportionate

sections.title: Schedule 2 Part 4 — Exemption: confidential references given by the controller
sections.summary: Personal data consisting of a reference given (or to be given) in confidence by the controller for the purposes of education, training, employment, or the provision of a service is exempt from the right of subject access. This means an employer who gives a reference is not required to disclose it to the employee about whom the reference was written.
sections.text:
The listed GDPR provisions do not apply to personal data consisting of a reference given (or to be given) in confidence by the controller for the purposes of—

(a) the education, training or employment, or prospective education, training or employment, of the data subject,

(b) the appointment, or prospective appointment, of the data subject to any office, or

(c) the provision, or prospective provision, by the data subject of any service.
sections.keyPrinciple: The exemption protects references GIVEN by the controller. A reference RECEIVED by an employer about a prospective employee is NOT covered by this exemption — only the referring employer benefits.
refs: UK GDPR Article 15 (right of access / SAR)
Schedule 2 Part 4 para 24 (management forecasting exemption)
Spring v Guardian Assurance [1995] 2 AC 296 (duty of care in references)
commonErrors: Assuming a reference received about a job applicant is exempt — the exemption only covers references given BY the controller
Withholding references given in confidence but failing to consider whether the identity of the referee can be redacted to allow partial disclosure

sections.title: Schedule 2 Part 4 — Exemption: negotiations with the data subject
sections.summary: Personal data consisting of a record of the controller's intentions in relation to any negotiations with the data subject is exempt from the right of access to the extent that the application would be likely to prejudice the negotiations. Directly relevant to settlement agreement negotiations, salary discussions, and contractual renegotiations.
sections.text: The listed GDPR provisions do not apply to personal data which consists of records of the intentions of the controller in relation to any negotiations with the data subject to the extent that the application of those provisions would be likely to prejudice those negotiations.
sections.keyPrinciple: Employers can withhold from SAR responses their internal negotiating positions on settlement agreements, pay reviews, or contractual terms — but only where disclosure would genuinely prejudice the negotiations.
refs: UK GDPR Article 15 (right of access / SAR)
Schedule 2 Part 4 para 24 (management forecasting exemption)
ACAS Code on Settlement Agreements

sections.title: Prohibition of requirement to produce relevant records
sections.summary: Makes it a criminal offence for an employer to require an employee, prospective employee, or service provider to make a subject access request and produce the resulting records as a condition of employment or service provision. Specifically targets the practice of using SARs as a backdoor route to obtaining criminal records or health data that should be obtained through legitimate channels (DBS checks, Access to Medical Reports Act 1988).
sections.text:
(1) It is an offence for a person ('P1') to require another person to provide P1 with, or give P1 access to, a relevant record in connection with—

(a) the recruitment of another person as an employee,

(b) the continued employment of another person, or

(c) any contract for the provision of services to P1.

(2) It is an offence for a person ('P2') to require another person to provide P2 with, or give P2 access to, a relevant record if—

(a) P2 is involved in the provision of goods, facilities or services to the public or a section of the public, and

(b) the requirement is a condition of providing or offering to provide any goods, facilities or services to the other person.

(3) It is an offence for a person ('P3') to require another person to provide a third person with, or give a third person access to, a relevant record if—

(a) P3 is involved in the provision of goods, facilities or services to the public or a section of the public, and

(b) the requirement is a condition of providing or offering to provide any goods, facilities or services to the other person.

(4) It is a defence for a person charged with an offence under this section to prove that the imposition of the requirement was required or authorised by or under an enactment, by any rule of law or by the order of a court, or was justified as being in the public interest.
sections.keyPrinciple: An employer commits a criminal offence by requiring an employee or job applicant to make a SAR and hand over the results as a condition of employment. The legitimate route for criminal records checking is through the DBS; the legitimate route for medical information is through the Access to Medical Reports Act 1988.
refs: Schedule 18 DPA 2018 (definition of 'relevant record' — criminal convictions, health data, statutory functions)
Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS) checks — the legitimate route for criminal records
Access to Medical Reports Act 1988 — the legitimate route for medical reports from a treating physician
s.56 DPA 1998 (predecessor provision — now repealed and replaced by s.184)
s.198 DPA 2018 (liability of directors for body corporate offences)
commonErrors: Requiring job applicants to obtain a SAR from a previous employer and produce the results as part of the recruitment process
Requiring employees to obtain a PNC subject access disclosure from ACRO as a substitute for a DBS check — this is the exact practice s.184 was designed to prohibit
Failing to recognise that the offence applies to continued employment, not just recruitment — asking an existing employee to produce SAR results is equally criminal

versions.text: Originally enacted as s.56 DPA 1998 but not commenced until 10 March 2015 (awaiting reforms to the Rehabilitation of Offenders Act 1974). Re-enacted as s.184 DPA 2018 on 25 May 2018.
versions.policyRationale: The commencement of this provision was delayed for over 16 years from the DPA 1998 because it could not be brought into force until the Rehabilitation of Offenders Act 1974 was reformed to narrow the categories of spent convictions that employers could legitimately require disclosure of.

sections.title: Schedule 1 Part 2 — Substantial public interest conditions for special category data
sections.summary: Schedule 1 Part 2 provides 23 conditions under which special category data can be processed where necessary for reasons of substantial public interest. Employer-relevant conditions include: preventing or detecting unlawful acts (para 10), protecting the public against dishonesty or malpractice (para 11), regulatory requirements relating to fraud (para 12), equality monitoring and anti-discrimination (para 8), occupational pension scheme management (para 16), and insurance (para 20). Each condition requires an Appropriate Policy Document to be in place.
sections.text:
Part 2 of Schedule 1 to the DPA 2018 sets out conditions for processing special category data under UK GDPR Article 9(2)(g) — processing necessary for reasons of substantial public interest.

Key employer-relevant conditions:

Paragraph 8 — Equality of opportunity or treatment: Processing of special category data (race, religion, disability, sexual orientation, age) is permitted where necessary for the purposes of identifying or keeping under review the existence or absence of equality of opportunity or treatment with a view to enabling such equality to be promoted or maintained. This is the condition that permits employer diversity monitoring.

Paragraph 10 — Preventing or detecting unlawful acts: Processing is permitted where necessary for the prevention or detection of an unlawful act and must be carried out without the consent of the data subject so as not to prejudice those purposes. This covers internal fraud investigations.

Paragraph 11 — Protecting the public: Processing is permitted where necessary for protecting members of the public against dishonesty, malpractice, seriously improper conduct, unfitness or incompetence, mismanagement in the administration of a body or association, or failures in services.

Paragraph 16 — Occupational pensions: Processing of special category data necessary for the purpose of making a determination in connection with eligibility for, or benefits payable under, an occupational pension scheme.

All Part 2 conditions require an Appropriate Policy Document (Schedule 1 Part 4 para 39).
sections.keyPrinciple: Employers conducting diversity monitoring, internal fraud investigations, or whistleblowing inquiries must identify the specific Schedule 1 Part 2 condition relied upon AND maintain an Appropriate Policy Document. The condition is not a blanket permission — processing must be 'necessary' for the stated purpose.
refs: UK GDPR Article 9(2)(g) (substantial public interest derogation)
Schedule 1 Part 4 para 39 (Appropriate Policy Document requirement)
s.10 DPA 2018 (special categories gateway)
Equality Act 2010 s.149 (Public Sector Equality Duty — provides lawful basis for equality monitoring)
commonErrors: Collecting diversity monitoring data without identifying the specific Schedule 1 Part 2 condition — paragraph 8 is the correct condition for equality monitoring
Conducting internal fraud investigations involving special category data without an Appropriate Policy Document
Failing to distinguish between Part 1 (employment condition) and Part 2 (substantial public interest) — different conditions serve different purposes and the wrong one may not provide lawful cover

sections.title: Enforcement notices
sections.summary: Empowers the Information Commissioner to issue enforcement notices requiring a controller or processor to take (or refrain from taking) specified steps within a specified period. Failure to comply with an enforcement notice is an offence. Directly relevant to employers who fail to comply with data protection obligations — the ICO can compel compliance.
sections.text:
(1) If the Commissioner is satisfied that a controller or processor has failed, or is failing, as described in section 149(2), the Commissioner may give the controller or processor a notice (an 'enforcement notice').

(2) The failures are—

(a) a failure to comply with any of the data protection principles,

(b) a failure to comply with a requirement imposed by Part 3 or Part 4,

(c) a failure to comply with an information notice, an assessment notice or an enforcement notice, or

(d) a failure to comply with any of the requirements or provisions listed in section 149(3).

(3) An enforcement notice may—

(a) require the controller or processor to take steps specified in the notice, or

(b) require the controller or processor to refrain from processing, or from processing personal data of a description specified in the notice.
sections.keyPrinciple: The ICO has the power to compel employers to comply with data protection law through enforcement notices backed by criminal sanctions for non-compliance.
refs: s.155 DPA 2018 (penalty notices)
s.157 DPA 2018 (maximum penalty amount)
s.148 DPA 2018 (assessment notices)
s.196 DPA 2018 (offence of non-compliance with enforcement notice)

sections.title: Penalty notices
sections.summary: Empowers the Information Commissioner to impose monetary penalties on controllers or processors who have failed to comply with data protection legislation. Penalty notices can impose fines of up to £17.5 million or 4% of worldwide annual turnover (whichever is greater) for the most serious infringements.
sections.text:
(1) If the Commissioner is satisfied that a controller or processor has failed, or is failing, as described in section 149(2), the Commissioner may, by written notice (a 'penalty notice'), require the controller or processor to pay to the Commissioner an amount in sterling specified in the notice.

(2) A penalty notice given in reliance on section 149(2)(a) may require the controller or processor to pay an amount not exceeding the standard maximum amount.

(3) A penalty notice given in reliance on section 149(2)(b), (c) or (d) may require the controller or processor to pay an amount not exceeding the higher maximum amount.
sections.keyPrinciple: The ICO can impose administrative fines of up to £17.5 million or 4% of global turnover for the most serious data protection breaches. This represents a significant enforcement escalation from the DPA 1998 maximum of £500,000.
refs: s.157 DPA 2018 (maximum amount of penalty — the standard maximum is £8.7m/2%, the higher maximum is £17.5m/4%)
s.149 DPA 2018 (enforcement notices)
UK GDPR Article 83 (general conditions for imposing fines)
s.160 DPA 2018 (guidance about penalty notices)
commonErrors: Assuming data protection fines are theoretical and never imposed on employers — the ICO has issued significant fines for employment-related breaches including Serco Leisure (biometric monitoring, 2024)
Failing to recognise that a data breach notification obligation exists under UK GDPR Article 33 — failure to notify within 72 hours is itself a fineable offence

sections.title: Compensation for contravention of the GDPR and this Act
sections.summary: Establishes the right to compensation for any person who suffers damage by reason of a contravention of the UK GDPR, Part 3, Part 4, or any regulations made under the Act. Expressly provides that 'damage' includes distress. This is the statutory basis for employee compensation claims for data protection breaches.
sections.text:
(1) In Article 82 of the UK GDPR (right to compensation), 'non-material damage' includes distress.

(2) Subsection (3) applies where—

(a) in accordance with rules of court, proceedings under Article 82 of the UK GDPR are brought by a person acting as a representative of a data subject, and

(b) a data subject does not wish to be represented.

(3) The data subject's wish not to be represented does not affect—

(a) the power of the court to make an order or give directions, or

(b) the application of rules of court in relation to the proceedings.

(4) A person who suffers damage by reason of a contravention of a requirement of the data protection legislation, other than the UK GDPR, is entitled to compensation for that damage from the controller or processor, subject to subsections (5) and (6).
sections.keyPrinciple: Employees can claim compensation including for distress alone for data protection breaches by their employer. There is no requirement to prove financial loss — following Vidal-Hall v Google Inc [2015] EWCA Civ 311.
refs: UK GDPR Article 82 (right to compensation)
Vidal-Hall v Google Inc [2015] EWCA Civ 311 (distress-only compensation)
Lloyd v Google LLC [2021] UKSC 50 (individual proof of damage required)
s.169 DPA 2018 (compliance with data protection legislation in defence)

leadingCases.name: Vidal-Hall v Google Inc
leadingCases.principle: Compensation for distress alone is available for breach of data protection law — no need to prove financial loss.
leadingCases.heldText: The restriction in DPA 1998 s.13(2) requiring pecuniary damage before non-pecuniary damage could be claimed was incompatible with EU law and must be disapplied. DPA 2018 s.168 now expressly provides for distress-only compensation.
leadingCases.significance: Directly led to DPA 2018 s.168 expressly confirming that 'non-material damage includes distress' — removing the previous requirement to prove financial loss.

sections.title: Unlawful obtaining etc of personal data
sections.summary: Creates a criminal offence for knowingly or recklessly obtaining, disclosing, or procuring the disclosure of personal data without the consent of the controller. Maximum penalty: an unlimited fine. Directly relevant to rogue employees who access personal data without authorisation, and to employers who fail to prevent such access.
sections.text:
(1) It is an offence for a person knowingly or recklessly—

(a) to obtain or disclose personal data without the consent of the controller,

(b) to procure the disclosure of personal data to another person without the consent of the controller, or

(c) after obtaining personal data, to retain it without the consent of the person who was the controller in relation to the personal data when it was obtained.

(2) It is a defence for a person charged with an offence under subsection (1) to prove that the obtaining, disclosing, procuring or retaining—

(a) was necessary for the purposes of preventing or detecting crime,

(b) was required or authorised by an enactment, by a rule of law or by the order of a court or tribunal, or

(c) in the particular circumstances, was justified as being in the public interest.

(3) It is also a defence for a person charged with an offence under subsection (1) to prove that—

(a) the person acted in the reasonable belief that the person had in law the right to obtain, disclose or procure the disclosure of the data,

(b) the person acted in the reasonable belief that the person would have had the consent of the controller if the controller had known about the obtaining, disclosing, procuring or retaining and the circumstances of it, or

(c) the person acted—

(i) for the special purposes,

(ii) with a view to the publication by a person of any journalistic, academic, artistic or literary material, and

(iii) in the reasonable belief that in the particular circumstances the obtaining, disclosing or procuring was justified as being in the public interest.
sections.keyPrinciple: An employee who accesses personal data on employer systems without authorisation commits a criminal offence. This is separate from any civil data protection breach.
refs: s.171 DPA 2018 (re-identification of de-identified personal data — also a criminal offence)
s.196 DPA 2018 (penalties for offences)
WM Morrison Supermarkets plc v Various Claimants [2020] UKSC 12 (employee criminal prosecution for data disclosure)
Computer Misuse Act 1990 (overlapping criminal liability for unauthorised access)
commonErrors: Failing to recognise that employees who snoop on colleague or customer records commit a criminal offence, not just a disciplinary matter
Not implementing adequate access controls — the Morrison Skelton case demonstrated that authorised access for one purpose does not authorise use for another

sections.title: Re-identification of de-identified personal data
sections.summary: Creates a criminal offence for knowingly or recklessly re-identifying personal data that has been de-identified (anonymised or pseudonymised) without the consent of the controller responsible for the de-identification. Relevant to employers who share anonymised workforce data for benchmarking, research, or reporting purposes — any recipient who attempts to re-identify individuals from the anonymised dataset commits an offence.
sections.text:
(1) It is an offence for a person knowingly or recklessly to re-identify information that is de-identified personal data without the consent of the controller who is responsible for de-identifying the personal data.

(2) For the purposes of this section and section 172, personal data is 'de-identified' if it has been processed in such a manner that it can no longer be attributed, without more, to a specific data subject.

(3) For the purposes of this section, a person 're-identifies' information if the person takes steps which result in the information no longer being de-identified within the meaning of subsection (2).
sections.keyPrinciple: Re-identification of anonymised data is a criminal offence. Employers who share anonymised workforce data must ensure recipients understand that attempting to identify individuals from the data is unlawful.
refs: s.170 DPA 2018 (unlawful obtaining of personal data)
s.172 DPA 2018 (re-identification: effectiveness testing conditions — provides a defence for testing de-identification processes)
UK GDPR Recital 26 (meaning of anonymisation)
ICO Anonymisation and pseudonymisation guidance

parts.title: Part 4 — Automated Decision-Making, AI in Employment, and DPIAs

sections.title: Automated decision-making and profiling in employment — including AI in recruitment
sections.summary: UK GDPR Article 22 gives data subjects the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects or similarly significant effects. DPA 2018 s.14 provides supplementary UK provisions on safeguards. In the employment context, this directly restricts: AI-powered recruitment screening without human review, algorithmic performance scoring that triggers disciplinary action, automated absence management systems that generate warnings, and AI tools that make redundancy selection decisions. Employers using AI in HR must ensure meaningful human involvement in any decision that significantly affects an employee.
sections.text:
UK GDPR Article 22(1): The data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or her or similarly significantly affects him or her.

Article 22(2): Paragraph 1 shall not apply if the decision:

(a) is necessary for entering into, or performance of, a contract between the data subject and a data controller;

(b) is authorised by Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject and which also lays down suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights and freedoms and legitimate interests; or

(c) is based on the data subject's explicit consent.

Article 22(3): In the cases referred to in points (a) and (c), the data controller shall implement suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights and freedoms and legitimate interests, at least the right to obtain human intervention on the part of the controller, to express his or her point of view and to contest the decision.

DPA 2018 s.14: Automated decision-making authorised by law: safeguards.

(1) This section makes provision about a decision which is 'a significant decision' — that is, a decision which produces legal effects concerning the data subject or similarly significantly affects the data subject.

(2) A controller may not take a significant decision based solely on automated processing unless the decision is required or authorised by law.

(3) Where a significant decision is taken, the controller must, as soon as reasonably practicable, notify the data subject in writing that a decision has been taken based solely on automated processing.

(4) The data subject may, before the end of the period of 1 month beginning with receipt of the notification, request the controller to reconsider the decision or take a new decision not based solely on automated processing.

(5) If a request is made, the controller must, before the end of the period of 1 month beginning with receipt of the request, consider the request and either notify the data subject of the steps taken to comply, or notify the data subject that it will not comply and the reasons.
sections.keyPrinciple: An employer cannot use AI or algorithmic tools to make employment decisions — including recruitment screening, performance evaluation, disciplinary action, or redundancy selection — without meaningful human involvement. 'Rubber-stamping' an automated output does not constitute meaningful human intervention.
refs: UK GDPR Article 22 (automated individual decision-making)
UK GDPR Article 35 (DPIA required for systematic automated processing)
UK GDPR Article 13(2)(f) (transparency — must inform employees of automated decision-making existence)
UK GDPR Article 15(1)(h) (SAR must include meaningful information about logic of automated decisions)
ICO guidance on automated decision-making and profiling
GOV.UK Responsible AI in Recruitment guidance (March 2024)
Equality Act 2010 s.13/s.19 (direct and indirect discrimination — AI tools can perpetuate bias)

leadingCases.name: Schufa Holding AG (CJEU)
leadingCases.court: Court of Justice of the European Union (Grand Chamber)
leadingCases.principle: Credit scoring by private companies constitutes automated decision-making under Article 22 where the score produced plays a decisive role in a subsequent decision. Meaningful human intervention requires active, informed, and independent judgment — not merely rubber-stamping an automated output.
leadingCases.heldText: The production of a score by a private entity, where that score is then used by a third party as a decisive factor in granting credit, constitutes an 'individual decision based solely on automated processing' within the meaning of Article 22(1) of the GDPR.
leadingCases.significance: CRITICAL — establishes that automated scoring which plays a 'decisive role' in a subsequent decision is caught by Article 22, even if a human technically makes the final decision. Directly applicable to AI recruitment screening tools where the algorithm's output is the primary basis for shortlisting.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Automated decision-making and profiling guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive ICO guidance on when automated decision-making falls within Article 22, what safeguards are required, and how to provide meaningful human intervention

regulatoryGuidance.publisher: GOV.UK (Department for Science, Innovation and Technology)
regulatoryGuidance.title: Responsible AI in Recruitment guidance
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: March 2024 government guidance specifically addressing AI in recruitment — DPIA requirements, bias audits, reasonable adjustments, and Article 22 considerations

commonErrors: Using AI recruitment screening tools that automatically reject candidates without any human review of rejected applications — this is prohibited by Article 22

Claiming 'human involvement' where a human merely rubber-stamps an automated output without independent assessment — the Schufa judgment confirms this does not constitute meaningful human intervention
Failing to inform candidates that automated processing is being used in recruitment — UK GDPR Articles 13 and 14 require this
Not conducting a DPIA before deploying AI-based recruitment or performance monitoring tools — UK GDPR Article 35(3)(a) requires DPIAs for automated decision-making with legal or significant effects
Failing to provide an effective mechanism for employees or candidates to contest automated decisions
Not monitoring AI recruitment tools for discriminatory bias — Equality Act 2010 applies to algorithmic as well as human discrimination

sections.title: Data Protection Impact Assessments — workplace monitoring and high-risk processing
sections.summary: UK GDPR Article 35 requires controllers to carry out a Data Protection Impact Assessment before processing that is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals. The ICO classifies systematic monitoring of employees, large-scale processing of special category data, and automated decision-making as high-risk processing that triggers the DPIA requirement. Employers deploying CCTV, email monitoring, productivity tracking, GPS vehicle tracking, or biometric systems must conduct a DPIA before implementation.
sections.keyPrinciple: A DPIA is mandatory BEFORE deploying workplace monitoring — not after. Failure to conduct a DPIA is a standalone compliance failure even if the monitoring itself would have been lawful.
refs: ICO DPIA template and guidance
ICO list of processing operations requiring a DPIA (October 2018)
UK GDPR Article 36 (prior consultation with ICO where risks cannot be mitigated)
ICO Employment monitoring guidance (October 2023)
Barbulescu v Romania [2017] ECHR 742 (six-factor proportionality test)

leadingCases.name: ICO enforcement against Serco Leisure (biometric monitoring)
leadingCases.principle: The ICO ordered Serco Leisure and associated community leisure trusts to stop using facial recognition technology and fingerprint scanning to monitor workers' attendance. The enforcement action found that biometric processing for time and attendance was disproportionate.
leadingCases.heldText: The ICO ordered the organisations to stop processing biometric data for the purpose of monitoring employee attendance, finding that less intrusive alternatives were available and the processing was not necessary or proportionate.
leadingCases.significance: CRITICAL — the first major ICO enforcement action on workplace biometric monitoring. Establishes that employers cannot use facial recognition or fingerprint scanning for attendance monitoring without demonstrating that less intrusive alternatives are insufficient.

commonErrors: Deploying CCTV, email monitoring, or productivity tracking software without conducting a DPIA first
Conducting a DPIA after deployment — the DPIA must be completed BEFORE processing begins
Failing to consult with the ICO under Article 36 where the DPIA identifies high risks that cannot be mitigated
Not reviewing and updating DPIAs when monitoring technology or practices change
Treating a DPIA as a one-off compliance exercise rather than a living document

sections.title: Age appropriate design code
sections.summary: Requires the Information Commissioner to prepare and issue a code of practice containing guidance on standards of age-appropriate design of relevant information society services likely to be accessed by children. While primarily consumer-focused, this is relevant to employers operating online services or platforms that may be accessed by young workers (aged 16-17) or by children as service users.
sections.text:
(1) The Commissioner must prepare a code of practice which contains such guidance as the Commissioner considers appropriate on standards of age-appropriate design of relevant information society services which are likely to be accessed by children.

(2) The code must be prepared and issued—

(a) in accordance with section 125, and

(b) before the end of the period of 18 months beginning when this section comes into force.

(3) In this section, 'relevant information society service' has the meaning given by regulations made by the Secretary of State.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Age Appropriate Design Code (published September 2020) imposes obligations on online services likely to be accessed by children. Employers running customer-facing platforms must assess applicability.
refs: ICO Age Appropriate Design Code (September 2020)
UK GDPR Article 8 (conditions applicable to child's consent)
s.125 DPA 2018 (procedure for issuing codes)

parts.title: Part 5 — Data Breach Notification and International Transfers

sections.title: Communication of a personal data breach to the data subject (Part 3)
sections.summary: Requires competent authorities to communicate personal data breaches to the data subject without undue delay where the breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of the data subject. For general (non-law-enforcement) processing, the equivalent obligation is in UK GDPR Articles 33-34 (directly applicable).
sections.text:
(1) Where a personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of any individual, the controller must communicate the personal data breach to the data subject without undue delay.

(2) The communication must describe in clear and plain language the nature of the personal data breach and at least—

(a) state the name and contact details of the data protection officer or any other contact point from whom more information can be obtained,

(b) describe the likely consequences of the personal data breach, and

(c) describe the measures taken or proposed to be taken by the controller to deal with the personal data breach, including, where appropriate, measures to mitigate its possible adverse effects.
sections.keyPrinciple: Employers who suffer a data breach affecting employee data must notify affected employees without undue delay where the breach poses a high risk to their rights and freedoms. Failure to notify is a standalone compliance failure and may attract separate penalties.
refs: UK GDPR Article 33 (notification of breach to ICO — 72 hours)
UK GDPR Article 34 (communication of breach to data subject)
s.68 DPA 2018 (recording of breaches)
ICO personal data breach reporting guidance
commonErrors: Failing to notify the ICO within 72 hours of becoming aware of a notifiable breach — the clock starts when the employer becomes aware, not when the investigation is complete
Failing to notify affected employees where the breach poses high risk
Not maintaining a breach register as required by UK GDPR Article 33(5)

pendingAmendments.instrument: Data Protection and Digital Information Act 2025 (anticipated)
pendingAmendments.status: The Data Protection and Digital Information Bill fell with the dissolution of Parliament in May 2024. The current Government has not reintroduced equivalent legislation as at March 2026. Any future data reform Bill could materially affect DPA 2018 provisions.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MONITOR — potential changes to SAR exemptions, automated decision-making provisions, and ICO enforcement powers

pendingAmendments.status: The immigration exemption in Schedule 2 Part 1 para 4 was amended by SI 2024/267 following the Court of Appeal judgment in Open Rights Group v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2021] EWCA Civ 1573 and subsequent further litigation. The amendment added additional safeguards required by UK GDPR Article 23(2).
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: LOW for most employers — primarily affects immigration enforcement processing

pendingAmendments.status: The Employment Rights Act 2025, when commenced, will strengthen employee whistleblowing protections including protections for workers who report data protection breaches. The Fair Work Agency may also exercise enforcement functions relevant to employee data handling.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — strengthened protections for employees who report employer data protection failings
